name: Dice

sourceRefs.label: civilserviceaspirants.in - 6 real past-paper dice/cube questions, sourced with exam-year tags (Group 1/VAO/Group 4, 2013-2025)
tnpscguru.in - dice probability questions, stashed earlier and used here
2025_q1 page 130 Q199 (opposite-face deduction, CCSE2-2AT/2025 exam, with answer key, image-verified)
2025_q page 116 Q125 (necessarily-true adjacency deduction, GST2AM/2025 exam, with answer key, image-verified)
2024_q page 60 Q91 (non-standard repeated-face die counting problem, CCSE2T/2024 exam, with answer key, image-verified)
2024_q page 30 Q43 (three-dice same-number probability, CCSE2T/2024 exam, with answer key, image-verified)
2026_q page 71 Q79 (probability axioms 'which statement is wrong', RE/GS2AM/2026 exam, with answer key, image-verified)

researchNotes: Strong evidence — the source page tagged each question with its actual originating exam and year (Group 1 2014/2024/2025, VAO 2014, Group 4 2013), giving real confidence these are genuine past papers even though they're hosted on a coaching aggregator rather than found directly in the local question bank. Two distinct shapes: cube-net 'opposite face' puzzles (three views → deduce a hidden pairing) and dice-probability questions (treated as a Dice sub-topic since they involve the same physical object, even though probability itself is a different mathematical skill). Expanded 2026-07 pass: re-scanned the OCR question bank directly (the original pass only had web-sourced hits) and found five genuine dice/probability questions across the 2024-2026 papers, each visually confirmed against the source page image (not just OCR text, which was ambiguous about which option carried the checkmark) before use. Two are opposite-face/adjacency deductions (2025_q1 Q199, 2025_q Q125) that generalize into the single most useful cube rule: any two faces that are NOT the stated opposite pair must be adjacent. One is a non-standard repeated-face-value counting problem (2024_q Q91, independently re-verified by hand: 5+6+0=11). One is a three-dice-same-number probability question (2024_q Q43, verified 6/216=1/36). One is a probability-axioms true/false question (2026_q Q79). A cube-net three-view image question on the same page as Q79 (2026_q Q78) was found but NOT used, since its pip-arrangement across three 3-D cube views can't be reliably transcribed into this app's text-only question format without risking a fabricated/misread layout — flagging per the 'skip rather than guess' rule. Rounded out with authored variants (four-dice-same probability, doublet probability, 'at least one six', letter-labeled die, sum=8) applying the same verified formulas with different numbers.

reading.intro: Dice is 1 of 6 named Reasoning skills. Real exam evidence (explicitly tagged by source year, 2013-2025) shows two distinct shapes: opposite-face deduction from multiple cube views, and dice-throw probability.

qa.question: Foundational rule: On a standard die, what do opposite faces always sum to, and why does this matter for solving these puzzles?
qa.answer: Opposite faces always sum to 7 (1-6, 2-5, 3-4) on a STANDARD die — but TNPSC dice puzzles often use lettered or non-standard-numbered faces specifically to stop you from relying on this shortcut, forcing you to actually deduce relationships from the given views instead.

qa.question: Method — deducing an opposite face from adjacency information: If face 2 is adjacent to faces 3, 5, and 6, what must be true about face 1's relationship to face 4?
qa.answer: Since 2 is adjacent to 3, 5, and 6, those three faces can't be opposite 2 — leaving only 1 or 4 as 2's possible opposite. With four faces (3,5,6, and whichever of 1/4 is also adjacent) surrounding face 2, the remaining face must be its opposite, and separately, on a standard 6-face die, this kind of constraint typically forces 1 and 4 to be adjacent to each other (since they're not the 2-pair) — real exam answer: '1 is necessarily adjacent to 4.'

qa.question: Method — three views of a cube → find the opposite face: Three pictures of the same cube show different combinations of visible letters/numbers. How do you find what's opposite a given face?
qa.answer: A face that appears on multiple different views, each time paired with DIFFERENT other visible faces, helps you eliminate possibilities — any face that has been seen adjacent to your target face (in ANY view) cannot be its opposite. Whatever face is never seen adjacent to it across all views is the opposite face.

qa.question: Method — probability of a sum from two dice: What is the probability of getting a prime-number sum when throwing two dice?
qa.answer: Possible sums range 2-12 (36 total equally likely outcomes from 6×6). Prime sums possible: 2,3,5,7,11. Count outcomes for each: sum=2(1 way),3(2),5(4),7(6),11(2) = 15 outcomes. Probability = 15/36.

qa.question: Method — basic single-die probability: What is the probability of throwing a number greater than 4 on a single die?
qa.answer: Numbers greater than 4 on a standard die: 5 and 6 — that's 2 out of 6 outcomes. Probability = 2/6 = 1/3.

qa.question: Real exam (2025_q1 page 130 Q199): In a dice, 1 is opposite to 4 and 2 is opposite to 5. Which of the following must be true?
qa.answer: Since 1-4 and 2-5 are the two given opposite pairs, only faces 3 and 6 remain — so they must automatically be opposite each other, the third pair. Face 2's ONLY non-adjacent face is its own opposite, 5; every other face (1, 3, 4, 6) must therefore be adjacent to it. So the exam's marked answer, '2 is adjacent to 3 and 6', is necessarily true.
qa.tip: Once two opposite pairs on a 6-faced die are known, the two leftover faces are automatically the third opposite pair — you never need a separate clue to find it.

qa.question: Real exam (2025_q page 116 Q125): If 2 is opposite to 3 and adjacent to 4 and 6, which statement is necessarily true?
qa.answer: The exam's marked answer is '1 is adjacent to 2 and 3.' The general reason: on a cube, a face is adjacent to every other face EXCEPT itself and its one opposite. Since 1 isn't stated to be opposite either 2 or 3 (their opposite is each other), 1 must be adjacent to both of them no matter which other face 1 itself is opposite to.
qa.tip: The single most useful cube rule: any two faces that are NOT each other's designated opposite pair MUST be adjacent — a cube has only 3 opposite pairs, so every other combination is automatically 'next to' each other. This answers 'necessarily true' questions instantly without drawing the cube.

qa.question: Real exam (2024_q page 60 Q91): A non-standard die has faces 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3 (repeated values). Two such dice are rolled (36 equally likely face-pairs). In how many cases is the 2nd die's face bigger than the 1st die's?
qa.answer: Count by matching VALUE-counts, not just values: die1 shows 1 (1 face) paired with die2 showing 2 or 3 (2+3=5 faces) → 1×5=5 pairs. die1 shows 2 (2 faces) paired with die2 showing 3 (3 faces) → 2×3=6 pairs. die1 shows 3 (3 faces): nothing on die2 is bigger → 0. Total = 5+6+0 = 11 pairs — the exam's marked answer.
qa.tip: When a die's faces repeat values, the total sample space is still 6×6=36 (each physical face counts as a distinct equally-likely outcome), but favorable counts must multiply the NUMBER OF FACES showing each value, not just compare the values themselves.

qa.question: Real exam (2024_q page 30 Q43): Three standard dice are thrown at once. Find the probability of getting the same number on all three.
qa.answer: Total equally likely outcomes = 6×6×6 = 216. Favorable outcomes (all three dice showing the same number) = 6 (111, 222, 333, 444, 555, 666). Probability = 6/216 = 1/36 — the exam's marked answer.
qa.tip: For 'all n dice show the same number', the formula is always 6/6ⁿ — favorable cases are just the 6 possible repeated-number outcomes, regardless of how many dice are thrown.

qa.question: Real exam (2026_q page 71 Q79): Which of these statements about probability is WRONG — P(A)>1, or 0≤P(A)≤1, or P(∅)=0, or P(A)+P(not-A)=1?
qa.answer: 'P(A)>1' is the wrong (impossible) statement — probability can never exceed 1. The other three are all valid probability axioms: probability always lies between 0 and 1 inclusive, an impossible/empty event has probability 0, and an event plus its complement always sum to 1.
qa.tip: Memorize the three axioms as a quick truth-check: 0≤P(event)≤1 always, P(impossible event)=0, P(event)+P(not-event)=1 — any statement violating these is automatically the 'wrong' one in a 'which is incorrect' question.

qa.question: What is the probability of throwing a 'doublet' (both dice showing the same number) when two dice are thrown?
qa.answer: 6 favorable outcomes (11, 22, 33, 44, 55, 66) out of 36 total = 6/36 = 1/6. This is simply the two-dice special case of the general n-dice 'all-same' formula, 6/6ⁿ.
qa.tip: Doublet probability with 2 dice is always exactly 1/6 — worth memorizing outright rather than recomputing.

qa.question: What is the total sample space size when throwing 2 dice vs. 3 dice, and why does this matter first, before counting favorable outcomes?
qa.answer: 2 dice → 6×6 = 36 equally likely outcomes. 3 dice → 6×6×6 = 216. Always compute this denominator FIRST — using the wrong sample-space size (e.g. forgetting dice are rolled together, or double-counting) is the single most common setup error in dice-probability questions.
qa.tip: Sample space size = 6 raised to the power of the number of dice thrown. Write this number down before counting anything else.

questions.prompt: On a standard die, the numbers on opposite faces always sum to:
questions.trick: Memorize the 3 standard pairs directly: 1-6, 2-5, 3-4 — each pair sums to 7.

questions.prompt: What is the probability of getting a sum of 7 when two dice are thrown?
questions.explanation: 6 ways to make 7 out of 36 total outcomes: 6/36=1/6.
questions.trick: Sum=7 always has the most ways (6) of any two-dice total — the count of ways decreases by 1 for each step away from 7 in either direction.

questions.prompt: What is the probability of throwing an odd number on a single die?
questions.trick: A standard die always splits evenly between odd (1,3,5) and even (2,4,6) faces, so both probabilities are always 1/2 — no counting needed.

questions.prompt: If face 1 is adjacent to faces 2, 3, 4, and 5 on a die, which face must be opposite face 1?
questions.explanation: Since 2,3,4,5 are all adjacent to 1, the only remaining face (6) must be opposite it.
questions.trick: Whenever 4 of the 5 'other' faces are confirmed adjacent to a target face, the 1 remaining face is automatically its opposite — simple elimination.

questions.prompt: What is the probability of getting a sum greater than 10 when two dice are thrown?
questions.explanation: Sums >10 means 11 (2 ways) or 12 (1 way) = 3 outcomes out of 36 = 1/12.
questions.trick: List only the extreme sums (11 has 2 ways, 12 has 1 way) rather than the full 36-outcome grid — sums far from 7 have very few combinations.

questions.prompt: Real exam (2025_q1 Q199): In a dice, 1 is opposite to 4 and 2 is opposite to 5. Which of the following must be true?
questions.explanation: 1-4 and 2-5 are opposite pairs, so 3 and 6 are automatically the third opposite pair. Face 2 is adjacent to everything except its own opposite (5) — so 2 is adjacent to 3 and 6.
questions.trick: The two unclaimed faces on a die are always opposite each other once two other pairs are fixed — no third clue needed.

questions.prompt: Real exam (2025_q Q125): If 2 is opposite to 3 and adjacent to 4 and 6, which statement is necessarily true?
questions.explanation: 1 is not opposite either 2 or 3 (their opposite is each other), so by the cube-adjacency rule, 1 must be adjacent to both.
questions.trick: Any two faces that are NOT the stated opposite pair are automatically adjacent on a cube.

questions.prompt: Real exam (2024_q Q91): A die has faces 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3. Two such dice are rolled (36 outcomes). In how many is the 2nd die's face bigger than the 1st die's?
questions.explanation: die1=1(1 face)×die2>1(5 faces)=5; die1=2(2 faces)×die2=3(3 faces)=6; die1=3: 0. Total = 5+6 = 11.
questions.trick: Multiply face-COUNTS per value, not just compare the values — repeated faces mean repeated counts.

questions.prompt: A non-standard die has faces 1, 1, 1, 1, 2, 3. Two such dice are rolled (36 outcomes). In how many is the 2nd die's face bigger than the 1st die's?
questions.explanation: die1=1(4 faces)×die2>1 (value2:1 face + value3:1 face = 2 faces)=4×2=8; die1=2(1 face)×die2=3(1 face)=1; die1=3: 0. Total = 8+1 = 9.
questions.trick: Shortcut: 'ties' (both dice same value) = sum of (face-count)² for each value = 16+1+1=18. The remaining 36−18=18 outcomes split evenly between '2nd bigger' and '1st bigger' since both dice are identical — so the answer is 18÷2=9, without separately totaling every value pair.

questions.prompt: Real exam (2024_q Q43): Three dice are thrown at once. Find the probability of getting the same number on all three.
questions.trick: Formula: 6/6ⁿ for 'all n dice show the same number' — for n=3, that's 6/216 = 1/36.

questions.prompt: Four dice are thrown at once. Find the probability of getting the same number on all four.
questions.trick: Same formula: 6/6ⁿ. For n=4, that's 6/1296 = 1/216 — don't stop at 1/1296, simplify the fraction first.

questions.prompt: Real exam (2026_q Q79): Which of the following statements about probability is WRONG?
questions.explanation: Probability can never exceed 1, so 'P(A) > 1' is the impossible/wrong statement; the other three are standard probability axioms.
questions.trick: Any option claiming probability above 1 or below 0 is automatically false — the valid range is always 0 to 1 inclusive.

questions.prompt: What is the probability of getting a doublet (both dice showing the same number) when two dice are thrown?
questions.trick: Doublet probability with 2 dice is always 1/6 — memorize this outright.

questions.prompt: What is the probability of getting a sum of 8 when two dice are thrown?
questions.trick: For two-dice sums, the number of ways peaks at 6 for sum=7 and drops by 1 for each step away from 7 in either direction (sum=6 or 8 → 5 ways, sum=5 or 9 → 4 ways, and so on).

questions.prompt: What is the probability of getting at least one '6' when two dice are thrown together?
questions.explanation: P(no six on either die) = 5/6 × 5/6 = 25/36. P(at least one six) = 1 − 25/36 = 11/36.
questions.trick: For 'at least one' questions, always compute 1 − P(none) instead of directly counting every 'at least one' case — far fewer terms to track.

questions.prompt: A die has faces labeled P, Q, R, S, T, U instead of numbers. If P is adjacent to Q, R, S, and T, which face must be opposite P?
questions.explanation: Since Q, R, S, T are all adjacent to P, only U remains — U must be opposite P.
questions.trick: With a lettered/non-standard die, don't reach for the 'opposite faces sum to 7' shortcut — just find the ONE face not listed as adjacent; that's the opposite.

questions.prompt: On a certain die numbered 1 to 6, face 5 is adjacent to faces 1, 2, 3, and 4. What must be opposite face 5?
questions.explanation: Since 1, 2, 3, and 4 are all stated adjacent to 5, the only face left — 6 — must be opposite it.
questions.trick: Whenever 4 of the 5 'other' faces are confirmed adjacent to a target face, the 1 remaining face is automatically its opposite — simple elimination, no need to visualize the whole cube.
